description: The iliaca fox sparrow was first noticed when I was looking at the mixed group of dark-eyed juncos I always have under my niger feeder. It was clear to me there was a bigger and plumper bird in there with the juncos. So I looked at it through my binoculars and saw it was a kind of sparrow, scratching in the ground and not just picking at the surface with its beak. From white colored breast to the flanks it was heavily streaked with thick rusty blotches with a central blob on the upper breast where the streaks came together. The gray head was also streaked rusty and on down to the back. The wings and especially the tail were rusty red. I then double checked the bird against the National Geographic Field Guide to the Birds of North America and confirmed that the painting was identical to the bird on my property. I then got it into my spotting scope at the lowest setting of 15x because it was so close. The fox sparrow was there intermittently all day and again the next morning. I saw it several times in good lighting.
